title: Chapter 15: The First Step
paragraphs: Three moons had passed since Yashil and Kirah had become something that no race in this world could call anything other than an absurdity: friends.

Kirah came to the ravine every week. Secretly, under the cover of night, bypassing demon patrols and elven watches. He brought food—not stolen, but found: wild roots, berries from blighted bushes that were poisonous to all races except demons and goblins. Each time, Yashil thanked him so sincerely that Kirah would look away and mutter something about "stumbling upon it" and how it "would have rotted anyway."
But on that day, something happened on the trade route that changed the course of Yashil's life forever.

A caravan. Seven wagons, loaded with grain and cloth. Elven merchants with a guard of four swordsmen. They were traveling along the Southern Tract when bandits leaped from the thickets on both sides of the road—a dozen thugs of mixed races: a couple of deserting elves, several feral half-orcs, and two humans with madness in their eyes.

Yashil saw it from his lookout post on the edge of the ravine. He saw the guards attempt to resist and how three of them went down in the first seconds—a knife in the back, an arrow in the throat. The fourth, a young ginger-haired elf, stood over the merchant's body, shielding him, but his face showed he knew the next blow would be his last.

Yashil gritted his teeth. Two thoughts clashed in his head. The first—familiar, ingrained in his blood after years of beatings: "Don't interfere. You're a goblin. No one asked you. You'll be the first to die." The second—quiet, stubborn, in his mother's voice: "Giving is not for fools. It is the strongest thing a living being can do."
He ran.

The goblin weighed less than a sack of grain and possessed neither sword nor magic. But he had something that no bandit expected: a total, absolute indifference to his own life when another's was at stake.

Yashil flew into the bandits' camp from the side, grabbed a burning branch from a scattered campfire, and, screeching at the top of his lungs—a goblin's screech can cause a migraine in a troll—pushed it into the face of the nearest thug. The half-orc screamed, clutching his face. A second bandit turned to the small green creature with a face contorted in rage.
"A goblin?! Are you serious?!" The bandit swung his sword.
And at that moment, a voice echoed from the bushes behind him—low, velvety, with the intonation of a being who truly does not care:
"Auf."

The bandit turned. From the thicket onto the road stepped a werewolf. Tall, lean, with a sharp wolfish muzzle and amber eyes in which a mockery flickered. A huge, jagged axe lay carelessly on his shoulder, and on his face—if you could call it that—shone a wide, fanged grin.
"Wolves aren't those who are in a pack, brother," the werewolf said, lazily scanning from one bandit to another. "Wolves are those who decide when the pack is over."
"Who the hell are you?!" the bandit leader barked.
"Janavar," the werewolf introduced himself, scratching behind his ear with a claw. "But friends call me 'Problem.' Well, they would, if I had any friends."

He attacked lazily—so lazily that it looked insulting. The axe described a leisurely arc and knocked out two bandits with a single blow from the flat side. Janavar knocked over a third simply by stepping on his foot. To the fourth, he yawned in his face with such force that the man dropped his knife in terror.
The remaining bandits fled.
Janavar watched them go, yawned once more, and turned to the small green creature standing in the middle of the road with a burning branch in his hands and eyes wide with wonder.

"Not bad, Greenie," the werewolf jabbed a claw toward the fleeing bandits. "You started it. That's respect. One real moment is worth a thousand promises, as they said in my pack. Well, before I left it. Because I am a lone wolf. Auf."
"You… why did you intervene?" Yashil managed to say, his knees still shaking.

"Why not?" Janavar shrugged his impossibly wide shoulders. "I was walking by. Saw a little kid throw himself into a crowd of thugs with a stick. Thought—he's either the toughest goblin in the world or the stupidest. Either way—it's content. I mean, worthy of respect. Seriously, brother, you're truly bold."
When the dust settled, something happened that Yashil did not expect.

The ginger elven guard—the only survivor—rose from his knees, clutching the wound in his side. He looked at the goblin. And in his face flickered something Yashil had never seen in an elf's eyes directed at a goblin.
Not disgust. Not contempt. Not fear.
Confusion. A lost, childlike, honest confusion.
"You… saved… us?" the elf said, as if the very construction of the sentence was physically painful for him.
"Yes," Yashil said simply.
The elf was silent. Then slowly, as if his hand weighed a ton, he held out his palm.
Yashil looked at it. The elf's palm—clean, white, with long aristocratic fingers. His own—green, rough, with traces of burns from the burning branch. He shook it.

It was the first handshake between an elf and a goblin on this road in the entire history of the world. An event so insignificant for the universe and so immense for the small green creature that the air seemed to become slightly warmer.
Janavar, watching this from the side, wiped his nose with a paw.
"Not crying. It's an allergy. To heroism," he grunted and turned away, blinking furiously.
